title: Function Overriding
description: A derived class redefining a base class function with the exact same signature — and why overriding is not the same thing as overloading.
objectives: Distinguish overriding (same signature) from overloading (different signatures)
Redefine a base class function in a derived class
Use the override keyword as a safety net
See why calling through a base pointer needs more than overriding alone

sections.text: 1. Overriding vs Overloading — Don't Mix These Up

rows: Same name, SAME parameter list and return type
Happens between a base class and a derived class
Compiler picks the version based on arguments
The derived class's version replaces the base class's for that derived type

sections.text: These two terms sound alike and are frequently confused, including in interviews. Overloading is same name, different signature, same class. Overriding is same name, same signature, base vs derived class.

Function overriding is when a derived class defines a function with the exact same name, parameters, and return type as one in its base class, replacing that function's behavior for objects of the derived type.
3. Overriding Works Directly on a Derived Object
Called directly on a Dog object, this is straightforward: Dog::makeSound() simply exists and is used, replacing Animal::makeSound() for anything that's actually a Dog.

Writing override after a function's parameter list tells the compiler "I intend for this to override a base class function." If the signature doesn't actually match anything in the base class — like this typo, makeSond instead of makeSound — the compiler reports an error immediately, instead of silently creating an unrelated new function and leaving the bug to be discovered much later.
5. What Happens Through a Base Class Pointer?

sections.code:
Animal *a = new Dog();
a->makeSound(); // WITHOUT virtual, this actually calls Animal's version, not Dog's!

sections.text: This is the catch overriding alone doesn't solve: without the virtual keyword on Animal's makeSound(), calling it through an Animal* actually uses Animal's version, even though a genuinely points at a Dog. This surprising behavior is exactly why Virtual Functions is the very next topic — it's the missing piece that makes overriding behave the way Polymorphism previewed.

sections.title: A specific job description replacing a general one
sections.text: A general "Employee" job description might say "greet the customer." A "Receptionist" job description, more specific, redefines exactly how that greeting happens for that particular role — same duty name, more specific instructions. When someone acts as a Receptionist directly, they follow the Receptionist version; the trickier question of what happens when you only know someone by their general "Employee" title is exactly what the next topic resolves.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing overriding with overloading — overriding requires an identical signature between base and derived; overloading requires different signatures within the same class.
Making a small typo in an intended override, creating an unrelated new function instead — the override keyword catches exactly this mistake at compile time.

Expecting a->makeSound() to always call the derived version just because overriding was written — without virtual on the base class's version, calling through a base pointer or reference uses the base version instead.
Forgetting the return type must also match exactly for a true override, not just the name and parameters.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is function overriding?
questions.answer: When a derived class defines a function with the exact same name, parameters, and return type as one in its base class, replacing that function's behavior for the derived type.

questions.question: What is the difference between overriding and overloading?
questions.answer: Overloading is multiple functions sharing a name with different parameter lists within the same class; overriding is a derived class redefining a base class function with an identical signature.

questions.question: What does the override keyword do?
questions.answer: It tells the compiler this function is intended to override a base class function, causing a compile-time error if no matching base function actually exists — catching typos and signature mismatches early.

questions.question: Does overriding alone guarantee the derived version runs when called through a base class pointer?
questions.answer: No — without the base class function being marked virtual, calling through a base pointer or reference invokes the base class's version, regardless of the actual object's type.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Function overriding happens when a derived class defines a function with the exact same signature as one in its base class, replacing that behavior for the derived type — distinct from overloading, which is same name, different signatures, within one class. The override keyword is a safety net: it makes the compiler verify a matching base function actually exists, catching typos immediately instead of silently creating an unrelated function. Overriding alone, though, doesn't guarantee the derived version runs when called through a base class pointer — that requires the base class's function to also be declared virtual, which is exactly what the next topic covers.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Function Overriding
sections.text: Overloading = same name, different arguments, same class. Overriding = same name, same everything, base vs derived. override keyword = a typo detector.
